preamble.summary: A pilgrim lifts his eyes toward the hills surrounding Jerusalem and asks where help will come from. The answer is immediate and absolute: from the LORD, maker of heaven and earth. The psalm then unfolds as a sustained assurance of divine protection — God does not sleep, does not slumber, guards every step, shields from sun and moon, and keeps the traveler from all harm, now and forever.
preamble.remarkable: The psalm pivots on a single question and answer in verses 1-2, then spends the remaining six verses elaborating that answer with escalating confidence. The verb shamar ('to keep, to guard') appears six times in eight verses — an extraordinary density that turns the psalm into a litany of watchfulness. God is not merely powerful; God is attentive. The negation in verses 3-4 is emphatic: the one who guards Israel lo yanum ve-lo yishan ('neither slumbers nor sleeps'). In a world where ancient peoples believed their gods slept (Elijah mocks Baal's worshipers with this in 1 Kings 18:27), this claim is radical. The God of Israel is perpetually awake, perpetually watching.
preamble.friction: The phrase 'I lift my eyes to the hills' (essa einai el heharim) has been romanticized in English-speaking devotion as a statement of finding comfort in nature. The Hebrew is more ambiguous — the hills could be the Judean hills where Jerusalem sits, but they could also be the high places where pagan shrines operated. The question 'where does my help come from?' may carry an edge: not from the hilltop altars, but from the LORD who made the hills themselves. The pilgrimage context supports this reading — the traveler is en route to Jerusalem and passing hills dotted with competing shrines.
preamble.connections: As the second of the fifteen Songs of Ascents (Psalms 120-134), this psalm moves the pilgrim from the distress of Psalm 120 (dwelling among hostile people) to active trust on the road to Jerusalem. The six-fold repetition of shamar anticipates Psalm 127:1 ('unless the LORD guards the city'). The declaration that God is 'maker of heaven and earth' (oseh shamayim va-arets) becomes a liturgical refrain in the Ascents collection (see Psalms 124:8, 134:3).

verses.text_hebrew: שִׁ֗יר לַֽמַּ֫עֲל֥וֹת אֶשָּׂ֣א עֵ֭ינַי אֶל־הֶהָרִ֑ים מֵ֝אַ֗יִן יָבֹ֥א עֶזְרִֽי׃
verses.text_kjv: I will lift up mine eyes unto the hills, from whence cometh my help.
verses.rendering:
A song of ascents.
I lift my eyes to the hills —
where does my help come from?
verses.expanded_rendering: The superscription shir lamma'alot ('a song of ascents') marks this as one of fifteen pilgrimage psalms sung by worshipers traveling up to Jerusalem. The Hebrew essa einai ('I lift my eyes') uses the imperfect tense, expressing habitual or ongoing action — not a single glance but a repeated lifting of the gaze. The hills (heharim) are the Judean highlands the pilgrim must cross. The question me-ayin yavo ezri ('from where does my help come?') uses the interrogative me-ayin, which expects a definitive answer. This is not doubt but a setup — the pilgrim asks in order to answer.
translator_notes: The definite article on heharim ('the hills') specifies particular hills — the hills the pilgrim can see, the hills on the road to Jerusalem. These are not abstract mountains but the terrain of the journey.
The question-and-answer format in verses 1-2 may reflect antiphonal singing: one voice asks, another responds. This liturgical structure was common in pilgrimage worship.

key_terms.semantic_range: song of goings-up, song of the stairway, song of the pilgrim climb
key_terms.note: ma'alot derives from alah ('to go up'). The term refers to the physical ascent to Jerusalem, which sits on elevated terrain. Pilgrims literally climbed to reach the temple. The fifteen Songs of Ascents (Psalms 120-134) were sung during this journey. Some traditions associate them with the fifteen steps between the Court of Women and the Court of Israel in the temple.

verses.text_hebrew: עֶ֭זְרִי מֵעִ֣ם יְהוָ֑ה עֹ֝שֵׂ֗ה שָׁמַ֥יִם וָאָֽרֶץ׃
verses.text_kjv: My help cometh from the LORD, which made heaven and earth.
verses.rendering:
My help comes from the LORD,
maker of heaven and earth.
translator_notes: The answer is fronted for emphasis: ezri me-im YHWH ('my help — from the LORD'). The title oseh shamayim va-arets ('maker of heaven and earth') is not decorative; it grounds the pilgrim's confidence in God's sovereignty over all created reality. The God who made the hills is greater than anything on them.

verses.text_kjv: He will not suffer thy foot to be moved: he that keepeth thee will not slumber.
verses.rendering:
He will not let your foot slip —
your guardian does not slumber.
translator_notes: The verb mot ('to slip, to totter, to be shaken') describes the danger of losing footing on mountain paths. The promise is viscerally physical: God watches the pilgrim's feet on uneven ground. The shift from first person ('my help') to second person ('your foot') may indicate a priestly or companion voice now addressing the pilgrim directly.

verses.text_hebrew: הִנֵּ֣ה לֹֽא־יָ֭נוּם וְלֹ֣א יִישָׁ֑ן שׁ֝וֹמֵ֗ר יִשְׂרָאֵֽל׃
verses.text_kjv: Behold, he that keepeth Israel shall neither slumber nor sleep.
verses.rendering:
Look — he neither slumbers nor sleeps,
the guardian of Israel.
verses.expanded_rendering: The particle hinneh ('look, behold') calls for attention — this is a declaration worth marking. The two verbs yanum ('slumber') and yishan ('sleep') move from lighter to deeper rest: God does not even begin to drowse, let alone fall asleep. The construction lo...ve-lo ('neither...nor') is emphatic double negation. The scope widens from 'your guardian' (v. 3) to shomer Yisrael ('the guardian of Israel') — the same God who watches one pilgrim's feet watches the entire nation. The claim directly counters ancient Near Eastern myths of sleeping deities. Israel's God maintains permanent, unwavering vigilance.
translator_notes: We render hinneh as 'look' rather than the archaic 'behold.' The Hebrew particle is attention-grabbing and conversational, not formal.
The escalation from yanum to yishan is deliberate. yanum is a light doze; yishan is deep sleep. The psalm denies both, from the lesser to the greater.

key_terms.semantic_range: keeper, watchman, guard, one who watches over, preserver
key_terms.note: From shamar ('to keep, guard, watch, preserve'). The root appears six times in this psalm (vv. 3, 4, 5, 7 [twice], 8), forming the psalm's structural backbone. A shomer in ancient Israel was a watchman stationed on walls or in fields — someone whose entire job was to stay awake and alert. Applying this to God asserts tireless, dedicated attentiveness.

verses.text_hebrew: יְהוָ֥ה שֹׁמְרֶ֑ךָ יְהוָ֥ה צִ֝לְּךָ֗ עַל־יַ֥ד יְמִינֶֽךָ׃
verses.text_kjv: The LORD is thy keeper: the LORD is thy shade upon thy right hand.
verses.rendering:
The LORD is your guardian;
the LORD is your shade at your right hand.
translator_notes: Tsilekha ('your shade') is not metaphorical luxury — for a pilgrim walking through the Judean wilderness under Mediterranean sun, shade is survival. The right hand (yad yeminekha) is the side of vulnerability in battle, where a warrior's sword arm leaves the body exposed. God positions himself precisely where the pilgrim is most unprotected.

verses.text_kjv: The sun shall not smite thee by day, nor the moon by night.
verses.rendering:
The sun will not strike you by day,
nor the moon by night.
translator_notes: The verb yakkekah ('strike you') is strong — the same verb used for military attack. The sun's assault is heatstroke, a real danger for ancient travelers. The moon's striking is less obvious to modern readers; ancient Near Eastern belief held that moonlight could cause illness, madness, or seizures (the English word 'lunatic' preserves this idea). The pairing covers the full cycle: day and night, the pilgrim is shielded.

verses.text_kjv: The LORD shall preserve thee from all evil: he shall preserve thy soul.
verses.rendering:
The LORD will guard you from all harm;
he will guard your life.
translator_notes: The word ra here means 'harm' or 'calamity' rather than moral evil — the psalm promises protection from danger, not from temptation. The parallel yishmor et nafshekha ('he will guard your life/soul') uses nefesh in its primary Hebrew sense of 'life, self, being' — not the Greek philosophical concept of an immaterial soul. God guards the whole person.

verses.text_kjv: The LORD shall preserve thy going out and thy coming in from this time forth, and even for evermore.
verses.rendering:
The LORD will guard your going out and your coming in,
from this moment and forever.
verses.expanded_rendering: The merism tsetekha u-voekha ('your going out and your coming in') encompasses all movement — departure and return, every journey and every homecoming. For a pilgrim, this is perfectly concrete: God guards the road to Jerusalem and the road home. But the phrase also transcends the immediate journey through the temporal expansion me-attah ve-ad olam ('from now and until forever'). The psalm ends by stretching the pilgrim's moment of trust into eternity. What begins as a traveler's prayer becomes a permanent theological claim.
translator_notes: The phrase 'going out and coming in' (tset u-vo) is a standard Hebrew merism for the totality of daily life. It appears in Deuteronomy 28:6, where God blesses Israel's going out and coming in as part of the covenant blessings. The pilgrim psalm echoes covenantal language.
